questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: According to Maslow's hierarchy of needs, which level must be satisfied before one can pursue love and belonging?

choices.text: Self-actualization
choices.explanation: Self-actualization is the highest level of the hierarchy — it must be pursued after love/belonging, not before.

choices.text: Esteem needs
choices.explanation: Esteem needs come after love/belonging in the hierarchy — they are built on the foundation of belonging.

choices.text: Physiological and safety needs
choices.explanation: Maslow's hierarchy requires that lower needs be sufficiently met before higher ones can be pursued — physiological (food, water) and safety needs must be met before people actively seek love and belonging.

choices.text: Cognitive needs
choices.explanation: Cognitive needs are not part of the original five-level hierarchy — the progression is physiological → safety → love/belonging → esteem → self-actualization.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Drive-reduction theory proposes that behavior is motivated by:

choices.text: The desire to achieve self-actualization
choices.explanation: Self-actualization is Maslow's concept — drive-reduction theory is specifically about reducing physiological tension arising from unmet biological needs.

choices.text: The need to reduce tension created by unmet biological needs
choices.explanation: Drive-reduction theory (Hull) proposes that physiological needs create drives (states of tension) that motivate behavior — eating reduces hunger drive, drinking reduces thirst drive, restoring homeostasis.

choices.text: The seeking of optimal arousal levels
choices.explanation: Seeking optimal arousal describes the Yerkes-Dodson law / arousal theory — not drive-reduction theory.

choices.text: Cognitive appraisals of a situation
choices.explanation: Cognitive appraisals are central to cognitive theories of emotion and motivation — not drive-reduction theory.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The Yerkes-Dodson law predicts that the optimal level of arousal for performance is:

choices.text: Always the highest possible level of arousal
choices.explanation: Very high arousal impairs performance — the Yerkes-Dodson law shows an inverted-U relationship between arousal and performance.

choices.text: At an intermediate level — neither too high nor too low
choices.explanation: The Yerkes-Dodson law shows an inverted-U curve — performance peaks at an intermediate arousal level. Very low arousal (under-stimulation) and very high arousal (over-stimulation) both impair performance.

choices.text: At the lowest possible level of arousal to minimize distractions
choices.explanation: Very low arousal is associated with poor performance due to lack of motivation — performance requires some arousal.

choices.text: The same regardless of task complexity
choices.explanation: The optimal arousal level varies with task complexity — complex tasks are best performed at lower arousal than simple tasks.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A student who studies psychology because they find it genuinely interesting and enjoyable demonstrates which type of motivation?

choices.text: Extrinsic motivation
choices.explanation: Extrinsic motivation is driven by external rewards (grades, money, praise) — studying because of genuine interest and enjoyment is intrinsic motivation.

choices.text: Intrinsic motivation
choices.explanation: Intrinsic motivation is driven by internal rewards — inherent interest, enjoyment, or satisfaction from the activity itself, without needing external incentives.

choices.text: Drive-reduction motivation
choices.explanation: Drive-reduction motivation involves reducing physiological tensions (hunger, thirst) — intellectual curiosity is an internal cognitive motivation, not a drive-reduction response.

choices.text: Homeostatic motivation
choices.explanation: Homeostatic motivation is about maintaining physiological balance — studying for interest is a higher-level cognitive motivation.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which of the following is a CORRECT description of Freud's id?

choices.text: The rational part of personality that mediates between id and superego
choices.explanation: This describes the ego — the id is the primitive, pleasure-seeking, impulsive part of personality.

choices.text: The moral conscience that enforces standards and produces guilt
choices.explanation: This describes the superego — the id has no moral awareness.

choices.text: The primitive, pleasure-seeking part operating on the pleasure principle
choices.explanation: Freud's id is the unconscious reservoir of instinctual drives operating on the pleasure principle — it demands immediate gratification without regard for social reality or morality.

choices.text: The conscious self-awareness that develops through therapy
choices.explanation: Conscious self-awareness is not a Freudian structural concept — Freud's id is entirely unconscious and not developed through therapy.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The James-Lange theory of emotion proposes that:

choices.text: Emotions and physiological arousal are triggered simultaneously by the same brain event
choices.explanation: Simultaneous triggering describes the Cannon-Bard theory — James-Lange proposes a sequence from physiological arousal to emotion.

choices.text: Emotion follows from physiological arousal — we feel fear because we notice our heart racing
choices.explanation: James-Lange theory proposes: stimulus → physiological arousal → awareness of arousal → emotion. We don't tremble because we are afraid; we feel afraid because we notice we are trembling.

choices.text: The cognitive label applied to arousal determines which emotion is experienced
choices.explanation: Cognitive labeling is central to Schachter and Singer's two-factor theory — not James-Lange.

choices.text: Emotions are entirely learned through classical conditioning
choices.explanation: Emotional learning through conditioning is behaviorism — James-Lange is a psychophysiological theory about the sequence of events in emotional experience.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Schachter and Singer's two-factor theory of emotion states that emotion results from:

choices.text: Physiological arousal alone, without any cognitive component
choices.explanation: This describes the James-Lange theory — Schachter and Singer specifically added the cognitive labeling component.

choices.text: Physiological arousal plus the cognitive label applied to that arousal
choices.explanation: Two-factor theory: emotion = physiological arousal (factor 1) + cognitive label that identifies the source of arousal (factor 2). The same arousal can become different emotions depending on how it is cognitively labeled.

choices.text: The cortical interpretation of stimulus information without any physiological component
choices.explanation: This is a purely cognitive view — Schachter and Singer retained physiological arousal as an essential component.

choices.text: The simultaneous activation of the cortex and body systems by the thalamus
choices.explanation: Thalamic simultaneous activation describes the Cannon-Bard theory — two-factor theory is about arousal plus cognitive labeling.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Paul Ekman's research on facial expressions across cultures found that:

choices.text: Facial expressions of emotion vary significantly across cultures
choices.explanation: Ekman found the opposite — basic facial expressions were recognized across even isolated cultures, suggesting universality rather than cultural variation.

choices.text: The same six basic emotions are expressed and recognized across cultures
choices.explanation: Ekman identified six basic emotions (happiness, sadness, anger, fear, disgust, surprise) with universal facial expressions recognized even in preliterate cultures — suggesting a biological basis for these emotional expressions.

choices.text: Emotional expressions are entirely learned behaviors
choices.explanation: If expressions were entirely learned, they would vary systematically by culture — Ekman's cross-cultural universality supports a biological, innate basis.

choices.text: Positive emotions are more universally expressed than negative ones
choices.explanation: Ekman found universality for both positive (happiness) and negative (anger, fear, sadness, disgust) emotions — the pattern is not skewed toward positives.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The facial feedback hypothesis suggests that:

choices.text: Facial expressions accurately reveal hidden emotional states
choices.explanation: Facial expression reading is a separate concept — the facial feedback hypothesis specifically proposes that making a facial expression can cause the corresponding emotion.

choices.text: Making a facial expression can influence the emotional experience
choices.explanation: The facial feedback hypothesis proposes that feedback from facial muscles influences emotional experience — forcing a smile can produce mild positive affect, while frowning can produce mild negative affect.

choices.text: Emotions are read most accurately in facial expressions
choices.explanation: Accuracy of emotion reading is a separate issue — the hypothesis is about the causal effect of making facial expressions on emotional experience.

choices.text: The face is the primary source of emotional expression in all cultures
choices.explanation: While facial expressions are universal, the hypothesis is specifically about the causal direction — that facial muscle feedback influences subjective emotional experience.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: According to Freud, defense mechanisms are used to:

choices.text: Strengthen the superego's moral judgment
choices.explanation: Defense mechanisms actually operate against the superego's demands — they protect the ego from anxiety generated by id impulses and superego guilt.

choices.text: Protect the ego from anxiety by distorting reality
choices.explanation: Defense mechanisms are unconscious psychological strategies used by the ego to reduce anxiety — they distort reality (through repression, projection, denial, etc.) to protect the ego from threatening id impulses or harsh superego demands.

choices.text: Allow the id to express its desires more freely
choices.explanation: Defense mechanisms typically block or redirect id impulses (e.g., sublimation channels them) — they don't allow free expression of id desires.

choices.text: Consciously regulate emotional responses to stress
choices.explanation: Defense mechanisms are unconscious — conscious strategies for managing stress are called coping strategies, not defense mechanisms.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A person who drinks heavily blames all their problems on their difficult childhood rather than on their drinking. According to Freud, this is an example of which defense mechanism?

choices.text: Regression
choices.explanation: Regression is reverting to earlier, childlike behaviors under stress — blaming one's childhood is not regression.

choices.text: Projection
choices.explanation: Projection is attributing one's own unacceptable feelings to others — blaming external circumstances is rationalization.

choices.text: Rationalization
choices.explanation: Rationalization involves creating self-justifying explanations to hide the true motives for behavior — attributing all problems to a difficult childhood while avoiding acknowledging the drinking as the cause is classic rationalization.

choices.text: Sublimation
choices.explanation: Sublimation channels unacceptable impulses into socially acceptable activities — creating external blame explanations is rationalization.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The Big Five personality traits (OCEAN) include all of the following EXCEPT:

choices.text: Openness to experience
choices.explanation: Openness to experience is one of the Big Five traits — it reflects intellectual curiosity and appreciation for new experiences.

choices.text: Conscientiousness
choices.explanation: Conscientiousness (organized, self-disciplined) is one of the Big Five traits.

choices.text: Authoritarianism
choices.explanation: Authoritarianism is not one of the Big Five traits. The Big Five are Openness, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, and Neuroticism (OCEAN).

choices.text: Neuroticism
choices.explanation: Neuroticism (tendency toward negative emotions, anxiety) is one of the Big Five traits.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Carl Rogers's person-centered therapy emphasizes which therapeutic condition as essential for growth?

choices.text: Confronting irrational beliefs through debate
choices.explanation: Confronting irrational beliefs is REBT (Ellis) or cognitive therapy (Beck) — Rogers emphasized acceptance and unconditional positive regard, not confrontation.

choices.text: Unconditional positive regard — accepting the client regardless of behavior
choices.explanation: Rogers believed unconditional positive regard (full acceptance of the person regardless of what they say or do) is essential for therapeutic growth — it provides a safe space for exploration and self-understanding.

choices.text: Analyzing transference relationships
choices.explanation: Transference analysis is a psychoanalytic concept (Freud) — Rogers's person-centered therapy did not focus on transference.

choices.text: Reinforcing adaptive behaviors
choices.explanation: Reinforcing behaviors is behavior therapy — Rogers's humanistic approach emphasized acceptance and self-actualization, not behavioral reinforcement.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Albert Bandura's concept of self-efficacy refers to:

choices.text: The unconscious belief that one deserves punishment
choices.explanation: Unconscious beliefs about deserving punishment are more related to Freudian concepts — self-efficacy is a conscious belief about capability.

choices.text: One's belief in their ability to succeed at specific tasks
choices.explanation: Self-efficacy is the belief in one's own capacity to execute the behaviors needed to produce specific outcomes — people with high self-efficacy set challenging goals and persist despite obstacles.

choices.text: The level of intrinsic motivation for a given activity
choices.explanation: Intrinsic motivation is whether one is driven by internal interest — self-efficacy is specifically about the belief in one's ability to succeed.

choices.text: The ego's capacity to mediate between id and superego demands
choices.explanation: Id/superego mediation is Freud's ego concept — self-efficacy is Bandura's social-cognitive concept about capability beliefs.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The Rorschach inkblot test is a projective personality assessment. What is a major criticism of this test?

choices.text: It is too difficult for most people to complete
choices.explanation: The Rorschach is easy to complete (just say what you see) — the criticism is about its validity and reliability, not difficulty.

choices.text: Low validity — responses are difficult to interpret objectively and reliably
choices.explanation: Major criticisms of the Rorschach include low inter-rater reliability and questionable validity — different scorers may reach different conclusions, and the test's ability to accurately diagnose personality traits or disorders is debated.

choices.text: It only measures conscious personality traits, missing unconscious content
choices.explanation: Projective tests are specifically designed to reveal unconscious content — the criticism is about validity and reliability, not missing unconscious content.

choices.text: It measures too many traits simultaneously to be useful
choices.explanation: The Rorschach is not criticized for measuring too many traits — the core criticisms concern its reliability and validity as a clinical tool.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which part of Freud's personality structure operates on the reality principle?

choices.text: Id
choices.explanation: The id operates on the pleasure principle — demanding immediate gratification regardless of reality.

choices.text: Ego
choices.explanation: Freud's ego operates on the reality principle — it mediates between the id's demands and the constraints of reality, delaying gratification until appropriate.

choices.text: Superego
choices.explanation: The superego operates on the morality principle — it enforces internalized moral standards and produces guilt for violations.

choices.text: Unconscious
choices.explanation: The unconscious is a level of consciousness, not a structural component — Freud's structural model consists of id, ego, and superego.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: Maria is a Type A personality. She is frequently competitive, impatient, and hostile, and works long hours even when she doesn't have to.

questions.question: Research on Type A personality suggests Maria may be at elevated risk for:

choices.text: Schizophrenia due to high stress
choices.explanation: Type A personality is associated with cardiovascular risk, not schizophrenia — schizophrenia has distinct neurobiological risk factors.

choices.text: Coronary heart disease, especially due to hostility
choices.explanation: Research links Type A personality (particularly the hostility component) to increased risk of coronary heart disease — chronic competitive stress and hostility promote cardiovascular wear.

choices.text: Depression due to overwork
choices.explanation: While overwork can contribute to burnout, Type A personality research specifically focuses on cardiovascular risk — the hostility component is most predictive of heart disease.

choices.text: Improved health outcomes due to high motivation
choices.explanation: Despite high motivation, Type A personality is associated with poorer cardiovascular health — the hostility and chronic stress response are harmful.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Bandura's concept of reciprocal determinism proposes that behavior is determined by:

choices.text: Genetic factors interacting with unconscious drives
choices.explanation: Unconscious drives are Freud's concept — Bandura's reciprocal determinism involves the interaction of personal factors, behavior, and environment.

choices.text: The mutual interaction among personal factors, behavior, and environment
choices.explanation: Reciprocal determinism proposes that person (cognitive factors, personality), behavior, and environment mutually influence each other in a bidirectional cycle — not a one-way causal chain.

choices.text: Reinforcement schedules in the external environment only
choices.explanation: Pure external reinforcement is Skinner's strict behaviorism — Bandura added personal cognitive factors to create a more complete model.

choices.text: The hierarchical structure of needs from physiological to self-actualization
choices.explanation: The hierarchical needs structure is Maslow's model — Bandura's reciprocal determinism concerns the interaction of personal, behavioral, and environmental factors.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which of the following is an example of the defense mechanism of projection?

choices.text: A person who hates their boss imagines their boss hates them
choices.explanation: Projection involves attributing one's own unacceptable feelings or impulses to others — projecting one's own hate onto the boss converts 'I hate my boss' (unacceptable) to 'My boss hates me' (external, less threatening).

choices.text: A person channels aggressive impulses into competitive sports
choices.explanation: Channeling impulses into socially acceptable activities is sublimation — not projection.

choices.text: A person denies being angry after a fight
choices.explanation: Denying an uncomfortable reality is denial — projection involves attributing one's feelings to someone else.

choices.text: A person creates logical reasons for an impulsive purchase
choices.explanation: Creating logical justifications for behavior is rationalization — not projection.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Research by Schachter and Singer demonstrated the two-factor theory by:

choices.text: Showing that facial expressions can create emotions without arousal
choices.explanation: Schachter and Singer studied arousal and cognitive labeling — the facial feedback hypothesis involves facial expressions, not arousal injection.

choices.text: Injecting participants with epinephrine and varying the cognitive label provided for their arousal
choices.explanation: Schachter and Singer injected participants with epinephrine (creating arousal), then exposed them to a happy or angry confederate — participants labeled their arousal as the emotion matching the social context, demonstrating that the cognitive label determines the emotion felt from arousal.

choices.text: Showing that animals experience the same emotions as humans
choices.explanation: Cross-species emotion comparison was not part of Schachter and Singer's study — they specifically tested the cognitive labeling component of emotion in humans.

choices.text: Measuring which physiological patterns are unique to each emotion
choices.explanation: Identifying unique physiological patterns per emotion describes research related to the James-Lange tradition — Schachter and Singer showed that the same arousal can become different emotions depending on the cognitive label.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Self-actualization, as described by Maslow, refers to:

choices.text: The achievement of high social status and respect
choices.explanation: High social status and respect describe esteem needs — self-actualization is about fulfilling one's full potential, not social recognition.

choices.text: Realizing one's full potential and becoming all that one is capable of becoming
choices.explanation: Self-actualization is Maslow's highest need — the desire to fulfill one's potential, pursue meaningful goals, and become the best version of oneself.

choices.text: Having all physiological and safety needs consistently met
choices.explanation: Meeting physiological and safety needs is the first level of Maslow's hierarchy — self-actualization is at the top, after all lower needs are met.

choices.text: The ego's ability to satisfy both id and superego demands
choices.explanation: Ego mediation is Freud's concept — Maslow's self-actualization is about fulfilling human potential, not structural psychodynamic balance.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A major criticism of Freud's psychoanalytic theory is that:

choices.text: It overemphasizes the role of consciousness in behavior
choices.explanation: Freud's theory actually overemphasizes the unconscious — not consciousness. A major criticism is that unconscious processes cannot be directly tested.

choices.text: Many of its core concepts are difficult or impossible to test empirically
choices.explanation: A major scientific criticism of psychoanalytic theory is that concepts like the unconscious, id, ego, superego, and defense mechanisms are difficult to operationalize and test empirically — making the theory resistant to falsification.

choices.text: It lacks any clinical application in therapy
choices.explanation: Psychoanalysis has clinical applications — the criticism is about its scientific testability, not therapeutic utility.

choices.text: It overemphasizes cognitive factors and neglects biological drives
choices.explanation: Freud actually overemphasized biological drives (libido, thanatos) — the criticism is about scientific testability, not neglect of biology.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The Minnesota Multiphasic Personality Inventory (MMPI) is an example of a(n):

choices.text: Projective test
choices.explanation: Projective tests use ambiguous stimuli — the MMPI is an objective self-report questionnaire with true/false items.

choices.text: Personality inventory (objective self-report measure)
choices.explanation: The MMPI is an objective personality inventory — a standardized questionnaire with true/false items, scored objectively, used to assess personality traits and detect psychopathology.

choices.text: Intelligence test
choices.explanation: The MMPI measures personality traits and psychopathology — not intelligence.

choices.text: Aptitude test
choices.explanation: Aptitude tests predict future academic or occupational performance — the MMPI assesses personality and mental health, not academic potential.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Freud's psychosexual stage during which the Oedipus complex emerges is the:

choices.text: Oral stage
choices.explanation: The oral stage (birth-18 months) centers on feeding and oral gratification — the Oedipus complex emerges during the phallic stage.

choices.text: Anal stage
choices.explanation: The anal stage (18 months-3 years) centers on toilet training and control — the Oedipus complex emerges in the phallic stage.

choices.text: Phallic stage
choices.explanation: The phallic stage (3-6 years) is when the Oedipus complex emerges — boys develop unconscious desire for their mothers and rivalry with their fathers, resolved through identification with the father.

choices.text: Latency stage
choices.explanation: The latency stage (6-puberty) is characterized by relative calm and social development — sexual impulses are dormant after Oedipal resolution.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The hunger regulation center in the brain is primarily located in the:

choices.text: Amygdala
choices.explanation: The amygdala primarily processes emotions, especially fear — hunger regulation is controlled by hypothalamic structures.

choices.text: Hippocampus
choices.explanation: The hippocampus is essential for forming new explicit memories — not for hunger regulation.

choices.text: Hypothalamus
choices.explanation: The hypothalamus contains the lateral (hunger stimulating) and ventromedial (hunger reducing) areas that work together to regulate feeding behavior — damage to these areas produces overeating or anorexia in animals.

choices.text: Cerebellum
choices.explanation: The cerebellum coordinates movement and balance — not hunger regulation.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A person acts friendly toward someone they secretly dislike. According to Freud, they may be using the defense mechanism of:

choices.text: Reaction formation
choices.explanation: Reaction formation involves behaving in the opposite way from how one actually feels — acting friendly toward someone you dislike converts the unacceptable hostility into its opposite (friendliness).

choices.text: Sublimation
choices.explanation: Sublimation channels unacceptable impulses into socially acceptable activities — acting opposite to one's feelings is reaction formation.

choices.text: Regression
choices.explanation: Regression is reverting to childlike behaviors — not acting opposite to one's true feelings.

choices.text: Rationalization
choices.explanation: Rationalization creates logical justifications — the person here is acting contrary to their true feelings, which is reaction formation.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which neurotransmitter is most associated with the experience of pleasure and reward?

choices.text: Serotonin
choices.explanation: Serotonin primarily regulates mood, sleep, and appetite — while it plays a role in well-being, dopamine is most specifically linked to pleasure and reward.

choices.text: GABA
choices.explanation: GABA is the main inhibitory neurotransmitter — it reduces anxiety but is not specifically the pleasure/reward neurotransmitter.

choices.text: Dopamine
choices.explanation: Dopamine is the primary neurotransmitter of the brain's reward system — released during pleasurable activities, it reinforces behaviors. The mesolimbic dopamine pathway (nucleus accumbens) is the brain's reward center.

choices.text: Acetylcholine
choices.explanation: Acetylcholine is primarily involved in muscle control, attention, and memory — not the primary pleasure/reward neurotransmitter.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The overjustification effect occurs when:

choices.text: Intrinsic motivation increases when external rewards are added
choices.explanation: The overjustification effect predicts the opposite — intrinsic motivation decreases when external rewards are added.

choices.text: External rewards undermine intrinsic motivation for an activity
choices.explanation: The overjustification effect (Deci, Lepper) shows that adding extrinsic rewards to an already intrinsically motivating activity causes people to attribute their engagement to the reward rather than genuine interest, reducing intrinsic motivation.

choices.text: Punishments increase motivation when they are sufficiently severe
choices.explanation: This describes punishment threshold effects — the overjustification effect is specifically about external rewards reducing intrinsic motivation.

choices.text: Drive reduction is insufficient to explain complex human motivations
choices.explanation: This is a theoretical critique of drive-reduction theory, not the overjustification effect.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Humanistic psychologists like Maslow and Rogers emphasize which aspect of personality?

choices.text: The unconscious conflicts driving personality
choices.explanation: Unconscious conflicts are the focus of Freudian psychoanalysis — humanistic psychology focuses on conscious experience and growth potential.

choices.text: Behavioral patterns shaped entirely by external reinforcement
choices.explanation: External reinforcement explains personality in behaviorism — humanistic psychology emphasizes inner experience and growth, not just external conditioning.

choices.text: Human potential, conscious experience, and the drive toward self-actualization
choices.explanation: Humanistic psychologists view people as inherently good, with natural drives toward growth and self-actualization — they focus on conscious experience, personal responsibility, and fulfilling potential.

choices.text: The stable traits that remain constant across all situations
choices.explanation: Stable traits across situations are studied in trait theory — humanistic psychology emphasizes growth, potential, and subjective experience.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which of the following is a key difference between trait theories and psychoanalytic theories of personality?

choices.text: Trait theories focus on observable, measurable personality characteristics; psychoanalytic theory focuses on unconscious processes
choices.explanation: Trait theories (Big Five, Allport) identify stable, measurable personality dimensions through self-report and observation — psychoanalytic theory emphasizes unconscious conflicts, defense mechanisms, and early childhood experiences that are not directly observable.

choices.text: Trait theories are older than psychoanalytic theory
choices.explanation: Modern trait theories (like the Big Five) are actually more recent than Freud's psychoanalytic theory — this is not the key difference.

choices.text: Trait theories believe personality is primarily determined by biological factors
choices.explanation: Trait theories identify stable personality characteristics without necessarily attributing them to biology alone — the key difference is focus on measurable traits vs. unconscious processes.

choices.text: Psychoanalytic theory has more empirical support than trait theory
choices.explanation: Trait theory actually has substantially more empirical support — psychoanalytic concepts are difficult to test and have received less scientific validation.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The Cannon-Bard theory of emotion differs from the James-Lange theory in proposing that:

choices.text: Cognitive appraisal must precede any emotional experience
choices.explanation: Cognitive appraisal as a prerequisite for emotion is central to the Lazarus theory — Cannon-Bard does not require cognitive appraisal.

choices.text: The thalamus simultaneously triggers both physiological responses and conscious emotional experience
choices.explanation: Cannon-Bard theory proposes that the thalamus routes sensory information simultaneously to the cortex (producing conscious emotion) and to the body (producing physiological arousal) — emotion and arousal occur together, not in sequence.

choices.text: Physiological arousal must occur before any emotion is felt
choices.explanation: Sequential arousal-before-emotion is the James-Lange position — Cannon-Bard argues for simultaneous occurrence.

choices.text: Emotion is entirely determined by facial muscle feedback
choices.explanation: Facial muscle feedback is the facial feedback hypothesis — not Cannon-Bard theory.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: In Maslow's hierarchy, which need is at the very bottom (most basic)?

choices.text: Safety needs
choices.explanation: Safety needs are the second level — physiological needs must be met first.

choices.text: Love and belonging
choices.explanation: Love and belonging is the third level — physiological and safety needs come first.

choices.text: Physiological needs
choices.explanation: Physiological needs (food, water, warmth, rest, shelter) form the foundation of Maslow's hierarchy — they must be sufficiently met before higher-order needs become motivating.

choices.text: Esteem needs
choices.explanation: Esteem needs are the fourth level — three lower levels must be addressed first.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which defense mechanism involves channeling unacceptable impulses into socially valued activities?

choices.text: Rationalization
choices.explanation: Rationalization creates logical justifications for behavior — sublimation involves redirecting impulses into constructive activities.

choices.text: Displacement
choices.explanation: Displacement redirects impulses to a safer target (kicking the dog after a bad day at work) — sublimation redirects them into positive, socially valued activities.

choices.text: Sublimation
choices.explanation: Sublimation involves redirecting id impulses (e.g., aggression, sexual energy) into socially acceptable or valued activities — a person with aggressive impulses becoming a surgeon is a classic example.

choices.text: Reaction formation
choices.explanation: Reaction formation involves acting in the opposite way from one's true feelings — sublimation redirects the impulse into something constructive.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Research on the role of the amygdala in emotion has shown that:

choices.text: The amygdala is responsible only for positive emotions like happiness
choices.explanation: The amygdala is most associated with fear and threat detection — though it plays a broader role in emotional processing, it is not specialized for positive emotions.

choices.text: The amygdala plays a key role in detecting threats and triggering fear responses
choices.explanation: The amygdala is a key structure in the limbic system involved in detecting threats, processing fear, and coordinating the fight-or-flight response — damage to the amygdala reduces fear responses to threatening stimuli.

choices.text: The amygdala suppresses emotional responses to prevent overreaction
choices.explanation: The prefrontal cortex is more involved in suppressing emotional responses — the amygdala amplifies and triggers emotional responses, especially fear.

choices.text: Removal of the amygdala eliminates all emotional experience
choices.explanation: The amygdala is important for fear and threat processing, but other brain structures also contribute to emotional experience — amygdala removal affects threat detection particularly, not all emotions.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Set-point theory of hunger proposes that:

choices.text: Hunger is entirely determined by willpower and conscious intention
choices.explanation: Set-point theory proposes a biological mechanism for weight regulation — not willpower.

choices.text: Each person has a biologically determined weight the body tries to maintain through hunger and metabolism
choices.explanation: Set-point theory proposes that the body has a target weight regulated by biological mechanisms — when weight drops below the set point, hunger increases and metabolism decreases to restore weight; when above, hunger decreases and metabolism rises.

choices.text: Hunger is driven purely by social and cultural factors
choices.explanation: Social and cultural factors influence eating, but set-point theory specifically identifies biological regulatory mechanisms for maintaining weight.

choices.text: Hunger motivation becomes extinguished once basic caloric needs are met
choices.explanation: Set-point theory explains why dieting is difficult — the body actively resists weight loss below the set point through increasing hunger and decreasing metabolism.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which of the following personality traits from the Big Five is most strongly associated with academic success?

choices.text: Extraversion
choices.explanation: Extraversion (sociable, outgoing) is less consistently associated with academic success than conscientiousness.

choices.text: Agreeableness
choices.explanation: Agreeableness (cooperative, trusting) is associated with positive social relationships more than academic achievement.

choices.text: Conscientiousness
choices.explanation: Conscientiousness (organized, disciplined, diligent) is the Big Five trait most consistently associated with academic and occupational success — high conscientiousness predicts goal-directed behavior, persistence, and achievement.

choices.text: Openness to experience
choices.explanation: Openness (intellectual curiosity, creativity) is associated with academic interests but conscientiousness is the stronger predictor of GPA and achievement.

questions.subject: ap-psychology

stimulus.content: A researcher measures participants' cortisol levels before and after viewing emotionally neutral vs. threatening images. Participants who have previously experienced trauma show significantly larger cortisol increases to threatening images compared to those without trauma histories.

questions.question: These findings are most consistent with which concept?

choices.text: The Yerkes-Dodson law
choices.explanation: Yerkes-Dodson concerns optimal arousal for performance — not differential biological stress responses based on trauma history.

choices.text: Sensitization of the stress response system following trauma
choices.explanation: Trauma can sensitize the HPA (hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal) axis — previously traumatized individuals show exaggerated cortisol (stress hormone) responses to threatening stimuli, consistent with PTSD-related hyperreactivity.

choices.text: The overjustification effect
choices.explanation: The overjustification effect concerns how external rewards undermine intrinsic motivation — not biological stress responses to threatening stimuli.

choices.text: Drive-reduction theory of motivation
choices.explanation: Drive-reduction theory explains motivation through physiological tension — it does not explain differential stress hormone responses based on trauma history.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: A child who throws a tantrum whenever they don't get candy is exhibiting behavior most consistent with which Freudian concept?

choices.text: Superego dominance
choices.explanation: A superego-dominated child would feel guilty and inhibit impulses — throwing tantrums for immediate gratification is id-dominated behavior.

choices.text: Id dominance — the pleasure principle demanding immediate gratification
choices.explanation: Throwing a tantrum to get immediate gratification — candy — is driven by the id's pleasure principle, which demands satisfaction regardless of social constraints or consequences.

choices.text: Ego functioning — using the reality principle
choices.explanation: Ego functioning would involve delaying gratification and finding socially acceptable ways to meet needs — not tantrums for immediate satisfaction.

choices.text: Sublimation of aggressive impulses
choices.explanation: Sublimation involves redirecting impulses into socially valued activities — a tantrum is an unsubllimated expression of frustration, not sublimation.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Person-situation controversy in personality psychology refers to the debate about:

choices.text: Whether personality or genetics better predicts behavior
choices.explanation: Nature-nurture is a separate debate — the person-situation controversy is specifically about whether behavior is better predicted by stable traits or by situational factors.

choices.text: Whether stable internal traits or situational factors better predict behavior
choices.explanation: The person-situation controversy (Mischel vs. trait theorists) debates whether cross-situational consistency in behavior reflects stable personality traits (person) or whether behavior is primarily driven by situational demands — research suggests both contribute.

choices.text: Whether psychoanalytic or humanistic theories of personality are more accurate
choices.explanation: This is a theoretical debate about personality models — the person-situation controversy is specifically about whether traits or situations drive behavior.

choices.text: Whether personality is conscious or unconscious in origin
choices.explanation: This question relates to psychoanalytic vs. cognitive theories — the person-situation controversy specifically concerns the relative predictive power of traits versus situations.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which hormone is primarily released during the fight-or-flight response?

choices.text: Oxytocin
choices.explanation: Oxytocin is associated with bonding and trust — not the primary hormone of the fight-or-flight response.

choices.text: Serotonin
choices.explanation: Serotonin regulates mood and sleep — it is not the primary hormone released in fight-or-flight.

choices.text: Adrenaline (epinephrine)
choices.explanation: Adrenaline (epinephrine) is released by the adrenal glands during fight-or-flight — it increases heart rate, dilates airways, redirects blood to muscles, and mobilizes energy for immediate action.

choices.text: Melatonin
choices.explanation: Melatonin regulates sleep-wake cycles — it is not released during the fight-or-flight response.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which of the following best illustrates the concept of displacement?

choices.text: A soldier returns from war and takes up marathon running
choices.explanation: Channeling aggression into athletics is sublimation — displacement redirects impulses to a substitute target, not into a socially valued activity.

choices.text: A person angry at their boss comes home and yells at their dog
choices.explanation: Displacement involves redirecting impulses from the original target (the boss — unsafe) to a substitute target (the dog — safer) — the emotion remains the same but is directed elsewhere.

choices.text: A person claims they are not angry when they clearly are
choices.explanation: Denying emotions is denial — displacement involves redirecting the emotion to a different target.

choices.text: A person creates elaborate justifications for hurtful behavior toward a friend
choices.explanation: Creating justifications is rationalization — displacement involves redirecting the emotion/impulse toward a substitute target.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Carl Jung's concept of the collective unconscious differs from Freud's unconscious in that it:

choices.text: Contains only repressed childhood memories
choices.explanation: Repressed childhood memories are in Freud's personal unconscious — Jung's collective unconscious contains inherited, shared human archetypes.

choices.text: Contains inherited archetypes shared by all humanity, not just personal repressed content
choices.explanation: Jung proposed the collective unconscious as a shared reservoir of inherited patterns (archetypes like the Hero, Shadow, Anima) derived from human evolutionary history — different from the personal repressed content of Freud's unconscious.

choices.text: Is entirely accessible through conscious introspection
choices.explanation: Jung's collective unconscious is unconscious — it influences behavior without being directly accessible to introspection.

choices.text: Is only present in men due to the Oedipus complex
choices.explanation: The collective unconscious is universal across humanity — the gender-specific Oedipus complex is Freud's concept.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: According to the table, which theory would predict that a person who notices their heart pounding during a first date would feel romantic attraction only if they attribute the arousal to the date rather than to exercise?

choices.text: James-Lange theory
choices.explanation: James-Lange theory proposes that awareness of physical arousal creates emotion — it does not include the cognitive attribution component that determines which emotion is felt.

choices.text: Cannon-Bard theory
choices.explanation: Cannon-Bard theory proposes simultaneous arousal and emotion triggered by the thalamus — it does not include cognitive attribution as a determining factor.

choices.text: Two-factor theory
choices.explanation: Two-factor theory predicts that the same arousal can produce different emotions depending on cognitive labeling — if the arousal is attributed to the date, attraction is felt; if attributed to recent exercise, the same arousal would not produce attraction.

choices.text: None of the above
choices.explanation: Two-factor theory (Schachter and Singer) specifically predicts that cognitive attribution determines which emotion is felt from a given state of arousal — making it the correct answer.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Rogers proposed that psychological problems arise when there is a gap between:

choices.text: The id and the superego
choices.explanation: Id-superego conflict is Freud's psychoanalytic framework — Rogers's humanistic theory involves a different concept.

choices.text: One's actual self and one's ideal self
choices.explanation: Rogers proposed that psychological distress arises when there is a large gap between the actual self (who you are) and the ideal self (who you want or think you should be) — therapy helps reduce this incongruence.

choices.text: Intrinsic and extrinsic motivation levels
choices.explanation: Intrinsic/extrinsic motivation conflict is related to self-determination theory — Rogers's incongruence concept involves the actual vs. ideal self.

choices.text: Environmental demands and one's personality traits
choices.explanation: Person-environment fit is a different concept — Rogers focused on the subjective discrepancy between actual and ideal self-concept.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Research on sexual motivation has found that unlike simple biological drives like hunger, sexual motivation:

choices.text: Is entirely determined by hormone levels with no psychological component
choices.explanation: Sexual motivation is influenced by hormones but also significantly shaped by psychological factors (desire, attraction, relationship context) — it is not purely hormonal.

choices.text: Is significantly shaped by external stimuli and psychological factors beyond biological needs
choices.explanation: Unlike hunger which is closely tied to caloric deficit, sexual motivation is heavily influenced by external stimuli, fantasies, relationship factors, cultural norms, and psychological state — making it more complex than pure biological drive-reduction.

choices.text: Only functions to ensure biological reproduction without variation
choices.explanation: Human sexual motivation serves social and emotional functions beyond reproduction — psychological and social factors shape it extensively.

choices.text: Disappears completely when basic physiological needs are unmet
choices.explanation: While extreme deprivation reduces sexual interest, sexual motivation can persist across many circumstances — it is not simply extinguished by unmet lower needs.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: The defense mechanism of repression involves:

choices.text: Redirecting unacceptable impulses toward a safer target
choices.explanation: Redirecting impulses to a safer target is displacement — repression involves unconscious exclusion of threatening material from awareness.

choices.text: Unconsciously blocking distressing thoughts or memories from conscious awareness
choices.explanation: Repression is the most fundamental defense mechanism — it involves unconsciously pushing threatening thoughts, memories, or impulses out of awareness. Freud considered it the basis for all other defenses.

choices.text: Consciously suppressing uncomfortable thoughts until they fade
choices.explanation: Conscious suppression is a deliberate strategy — repression is unconscious and automatic, occurring without intentional effort.

choices.text: Behaving opposite to one's true feelings
choices.explanation: Behaving opposite to true feelings is reaction formation — repression specifically involves blocking distressing content from conscious awareness.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which finding would most directly challenge drive-reduction theory as a complete explanation of motivation?

choices.text: Animals and humans seek food when hungry
choices.explanation: Seeking food when hungry is exactly what drive-reduction theory predicts — it does not challenge the theory.

choices.text: Rats will explore a maze for no external reward and with no biological deprivation
choices.explanation: If motivation were purely about reducing biological drives, organisms with no unmet needs would not explore. The finding that rats spontaneously explore novel environments — demonstrating curiosity or intrinsic motivation — challenges drive-reduction theory.

choices.text: Hunger drives eating behavior in animals
choices.explanation: Drive-reduction theory readily explains hunger-driven eating — this finding supports, not challenges, the theory.

choices.text: Organisms learn to associate neutral stimuli with reward
choices.explanation: Classical conditioning of neutral stimuli is a learning phenomenon — it does not directly address or challenge the drive-reduction account of motivation.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Which of the following is an example of an incentive (external) theory of motivation?

choices.text: A person eats because they are hungry and need to restore homeostasis
choices.explanation: Eating due to hunger and homeostasis is drive-reduction theory — incentive theory concerns external stimuli that pull behavior.

choices.text: A person works hard in class to earn a good grade (external reward)
choices.explanation: Incentive theory proposes that behavior is motivated by external goals or rewards that pull behavior — working hard for a grade is motivated by an external incentive rather than an internal biological need.

choices.text: A person studies because they love learning and find it intrinsically rewarding
choices.explanation: Studying out of love of learning is intrinsic motivation — incentive theory specifically refers to external stimuli that motivate behavior.

choices.text: A person explores a new city out of curiosity with no particular goal
choices.explanation: Exploration driven by curiosity is more consistent with arousal theory or intrinsic motivation — incentive theory involves specific external rewards pulling behavior.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: According to Freud, a person who remained excessively dependent and demanding as an adult may be showing signs of fixation at the:

choices.text: Phallic stage
choices.explanation: Phallic stage fixation is associated with vanity, recklessness, or sexual problems — not excessive dependence and demandingness.

choices.text: Oral stage
choices.explanation: Freud proposed that fixation at the oral stage (birth-18 months) — due to underfeeding or overfeeding — produces an adult who is excessively dependent, needy, or demanding — reflecting unresolved oral needs.

choices.text: Anal stage
choices.explanation: Anal stage fixation produces rigidity, orderliness, and control issues — not dependence and demandingness.

choices.text: Latency stage
choices.explanation: The latency stage is relatively conflict-free — fixation there would not produce strong dependent personality patterns.

questions.subject: ap-psychology
questions.question: Research across cultures consistently shows which emotional experience is associated with the highest subjective well-being?

choices.text: High intensity of both positive and negative emotions
choices.explanation: High intensity negative emotions reduce well-being — the pattern associated with highest well-being is frequent positive affect.

choices.text: Frequent positive emotions and infrequent negative emotions
choices.explanation: Cross-cultural research on subjective well-being consistently finds that people who experience positive emotions frequently and negative emotions infrequently report the highest life satisfaction and well-being.

choices.text: Complete emotional neutrality and absence of strong feeling
choices.explanation: Emotional flatness is associated with depression and low well-being — not high well-being.

choices.text: Primarily negative emotions interpreted as growth-promoting challenges
choices.explanation: While growth mindset matters, the emotional pattern most associated with well-being is frequent positive affect, not frequent negative emotions regardless of framing.
